purpose: Show full skip-counting-to-equation pipeline with explicit vocabulary naming.
visual: Context Visualization: 7 bags with 2 items each (bags maintain context continuity from Warmup; value changed from 5×2 to avoid overuse — 5×2=10 appeared in W.2, 1.1, and 1.3). Equation Builder appears with expanded template: `[___] × [___] = [___]`. Three slots visible. = sign is fixed (not a tile). First two slots pre-filled with 7 and 2.
guide: [THINK-ALOUD] "You discovered that multiples of 2 are always even. Now let me show you something new. I already know the expression: 7 times 2. But now I want to find what it EQUALS — the total."
guide_2: "First, I ask: what does this mean? 7 groups of 2."
visual_3: As Guide counts, bags highlight sequentially. Skip-count numbers appear briefly above each bag.
visual_4: Guide places 14 in the product slot. Complete equation: `7 × 2 = 14`
guide_5: "The whole thing — 7 times 2 equals 14 — is called an EQUATION. You've seen expressions before: 7 times 2. An equation has the equals sign and the answer."
guide_6: "That answer — 14 — is the PRODUCT. You learned that word before: the product is the answer to a multiplication problem. And the numbers being multiplied — 7 and 2 — those are called FACTORS. Factor times factor equals product."
visual_5: Labels appear briefly: "factor" under 7, "factor" under 2, "product" under 14.
vocabulary_note: Three terms staged in one natural moment: "equation" (new, names what student sees), "product" (reinforced from M7 — "You learned that word before"), "factor" (new, names the parts). All attach to the visible equation on screen. Per = sign language guidance: Guide says "equals" naturally when reading the equation. Does NOT define = or explain "same value as." Value changed from 5×2=10 to 7×2=14 to avoid overuse of 10 as product; 7×2 also demonstrates a longer skip-count sequence (7 jumps), making the strategy more visible as a worked example.
